question: What is the fundamental concept behind Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods, and name two common MCMC algorithms.
expectedPoints: MCMC is a class of algorithms used to sample from a complex probability distribution when direct sampling is difficult.
The core idea is to construct a Markov chain that has the desired distribution as its equilibrium distribution.
Two common algorithms are the Metropolis-Hastings algorithm and the Gibbs sampler.

question: Explain the role of the proposal distribution in the Metropolis-Hastings algorithm and how the acceptance probability ensures the chain converges to the target distribution.
expectedPoints: The proposal distribution (q) suggests a new state (x*) given the current state (x). It is often a simple distribution like a Gaussian centered on x.
The acceptance probability, α = min(1, [p(x*)q(x|x*)] / [p(x)q(x*|x)]), determines whether the proposed state is accepted.
This acceptance ratio ensures the chain satisfies detailed balance, which is a sufficient condition for the chain to have the target distribution p(x) as its stationary distribution.

question: Describe the process of Gibbs sampling. Detail its key assumptions, the steps of a single iteration, and discuss one major advantage and one limitation compared to the Metropolis-Hastings algorithm.
expectedPoints: Gibbs sampling is an MCMC algorithm used when the joint distribution p(x) is hard to sample from, but the conditional distributions for each variable p(x_i | x_{-i}) are known and easy to sample from.

An iteration involves sampling each variable in turn from its conditional distribution, holding all other variables fixed at their current values: x_1^(t+1) ~ p(x_1 | x_2^(t), x_3^(t), ...), then x_2^(t+1) ~ p(x_2 | x_1^(t+1), x_3^(t), ...), etc.
A key advantage is that its acceptance rate is 1; every proposed sample is accepted, making it often more efficient than Metropolis-Hastings.
A major limitation is that it requires being able to sample directly from all full conditional distributions, which is not always possible. It can also mix slowly if variables are highly correlated.
